title: Lake Winnibigoshish Land Exchange Act of 2025
short_title: Lake Winnibigoshish Land Exchange Act of 2025
stage_label: Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry. Reported by Senator Boozman without amendment. Without written report
plain_summary: This bill lets the Forest Service trade federal land for private land near Lake Winnibigoshish in Minnesota. The government will give about seventeen acres to Big Winnie Land and Timber LLC and get about thirty-seven acres back that will become part of the Chippewa National Forest. Before the trade happens, the company must do environmental tests and get professional appraisals to make sure both pieces of land are worth the same amount. The company also has to pay all the costs for completing the land swap.
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 218.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Lake Winnibigoshish Land Exchange Act of twenty twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these terms mean the following. BWLT means Big Winnie Land and Timber, LLC, which is a Minnesota Limited Liability Corporation (a type of business). Map means the map called Heig Land Exchange that was made on December fourteenth, twenty twenty-three. Federal land means about seventeen and a half acres of land owned by the federal government in Itasca County, Minnesota, shown as the Federal Parcel on the Map. Non-federal land means about thirty-six and seven-tenths acres of land not owned by the federal government in Itasca County, Minnesota, shown as the Non-Federal Parcel on the Map. Secretary means the Secretary of Agriculture, working through the Chief of the Forest Service.

sections.title: Section 3 Exchange of Land
sections.text: The government can trade land with BWLT if certain rules are followed. If BWLT offers to give their non-federal land to the United States, the Secretary must do three things within one year. First, accept the offer. Second, give BWLT all ownership rights to the federal land, but keep the right to use a road to reach other National Forest land to the west. Third, accept all ownership rights to the non-federal land from BWLT. The trade must meet several requirements. BWLT must prove they legally own the non-federal land in a way the Secretary approves. If the federal land is worth more than the non-federal land based on professional appraisals, BWLT must pay the United States the difference in cash. BWLT must complete and provide an environmental study called a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment of the non-federal land before the trade happens. The trade must respect any existing legal rights other people may have to use the land. The Secretary can add other conditions they think are needed. The trade should be for land of equal value, or the values must be made equal with a cash payment. However, if the non-federal land is worth more than the federal land, the United States does not have to pay BWLT the difference. Instead, BWLT is considered to be donating that extra value to the United States. The value of both pieces of land must be determined by independent professional appraisers that both the Secretary and BWLT agree to use. These appraisals must follow the Uniform Appraisal Standards for Federal Land Acquisitions and the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice. BWLT must prove they legally own the non-federal land according to federal law found in section thirty-one eleven of title forty of the United States Code. Once the United States gets the non-federal land, it will become part of the Chippewa National Forest and will be managed like all other National Forest land according to forest laws, rules, and regulations. As soon as possible after this law is passed, the Secretary must finish the Map and create legal descriptions of all land involved in the trade. If there are any differences between the Map and the legal descriptions, the Map is the official version. The Secretary and BWLT can work together to fix any small mistakes in the Map or legal descriptions, including mistakes about where the boundaries of the land are located. The Map and legal descriptions must be kept in Forest Service offices where the public can look at them. BWLT must pay all costs related to completing the land trade. These costs include title insurance and title searches, inspection fees, escrow fees, attorneys fees, and recording fees. BWLT must also pay for any environmental studies or resource surveys required by federal law, regulation, or policy, including the Phase I Environmental Site Assessment of the non-federal land. The exact size and legal descriptions of both the federal and non-federal land must be determined by land surveys that the Secretary finds acceptable. BWLT must pay for all costs of these surveys.
